---
title: "Data Visualization"
subtitle: "Learning R with Dr. Hu and His Friends Workshop Series"
author: "Yue Hu"
institute: "Department of Political Science, THU"

webr:
  packages:
    - drhur
    - drhutools
    - dplyr
    - tidyr
    - ggplot2
    - scales
    - lattice
    - ggside
---

{{< include _extensions/r-wasm/live/_knitr.qmd >}}

## Outline


1. Base graphics
2. Trellis graphics
3. Grammar of graphics

::: {.fragment}

*Research question*: What is the relationship between income level and egalitarian beliefs?

:::


---

- Visualization is one of R's signature strengths, still unmatched
- Three common approaches: base graphics / lattice (trellis) / ggplot2 (grammar of graphics)
- Variable coding: `equalIncentive` 1 = fully toward equality of outcome, 10 = fully toward equality of opportunity
- Positive hypothesis: higher income leans toward equality of opportunity
- Negative hypothesis: lower income craves equality of opportunity

## Base Graphics

- R ships with a base graphics system: functions act directly on the graphics device, simple and fast
- Logic: draw step by step, like a painter stroke by stroke — function order = layer order
- Scatter plot function: `plot(<x-variable>, <y-variable>)`

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: basicPlot

# Load data and fix the random seed (so jitter results are reproducible)
data("wvs7", package = "drhur")
set.seed(313)

# Pre-generate gender labels for later faceting/grouping
wvs7$female_label <- factor(wvs7$female, levels = c(0, 1), labels = c("Male", "Female"))

# Basic scatter: income on x-axis, egalitarianism on y-axis
plot(wvs7$incomeLevel, wvs7$equalIncentive)
```

:::

---

- Both variables are integers; points overlap heavily, masking information
- Solution: `jitter()` adds a small random offset to spread overlapping points apart

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: jitterPlot

# Use plot, but add jitter to the data points
plot(
  # Add a 0.3 random offset on the x-axis to avoid overlap
  jitter(wvs7$incomeLevel, amount = 0.3),
  # Add a 0.3 random offset on the y-axis
  jitter(wvs7$equalIncentive, amount = 0.3),
  # Set axis labels
  xlab = "Income level",
  ylab = "Egalitarian belief (outcome -> opportunity)"
)
```

:::

---

- Add a fit line to judge trend: positive slope (low-left to high-right) = positive correlation
- `lm()` fits a linear model; `abline()` draws the model as a straight line

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: lmPlot

# Draw the base scatter
plot(
  jitter(wvs7$incomeLevel, amount = 0.3),
  jitter(wvs7$equalIncentive, amount = 0.3),
  xlab = "Income level",
  ylab = "Egalitarian belief (outcome -> opportunity)"
)

# Add a linear regression fit line
# lm() fits the model, abline() plots it, col sets the color
abline(lm(equalIncentive ~ incomeLevel, data = wvs7), col = "gold")
```

:::

---

- The gold line rises from left to right (positive slope) → higher income leans toward equality of opportunity
- But the line is shallow: income affects egalitarian views, yet not dramatically

---

::: {.callout-important title="Theory vs. Practice" .fragment}

- Tutorial examples often show crisp visual patterns; continuous data are uncommon in social sciences and humanities
- Plotting discrete data with continuous-data methods often yields the messy look of the initial plot
- This is the norm in new-liberal-arts research; set realistic expectations, then use techniques to mitigate

:::

- Counter-example: a clean relationship between continuous variables (horsepower vs. mileage, clearly negative)

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: cleanExample

# Scatter plot between continuous variables
plot(mtcars$hp, mtcars$mpg, xlab = "Horsepower", ylab = "Miles per gallon")
```

:::

---

- Another trick for discrete-as-continuous: kernel-density smoothing (covered last chapter)
- `smoothScatter()`: color by density — darker where points cluster, lighter where they thin out

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: smoothScatterPlot

# Smooth scatter: use color shade to represent density
smoothScatter(
  wvs7$incomeLevel,
  wvs7$equalIncentive,
  xlab = "Income level",
  ylab = "Egalitarian belief (outcome -> opportunity)"
)
```

:::

---

- Save a plot in three steps (PNG example), mnemonic "lay paper — paint — collect paper":
    - Lay paper: `png("filename")` opens the graphics device
    - Paint: draw as usual
    - Collect paper: `dev.off()` closes the device and saves
- R supports many formats: `.jpg`, `.png`, `.pdf`, `.bmp`, etc.

---

```r
# Lay paper: open a PNG device, specify the filename
png("plot-incomeEquality.png")

# Paint: draw the scatter
plot(
  jitter(wvs7$incomeLevel, amount = 0.3),
  jitter(wvs7$equalIncentive, amount = 0.3),
  xlab = "Income level",
  ylab = "Egalitarian belief (outcome -> opportunity)"
)
# Decorate: add a fit line
abline(lm(equalIncentive ~ incomeLevel, data = wvs7), col = "gold")

# Collect paper: close the device, save the file
dev.off()
```

---

- Multi-panel comparison: `par(mfrow = c(1, 2))` splits the canvas into 1 row × 2 columns
- Draw male and female scatters with fit lines, place side by side to compare

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: sexPlot

# Split data: extract male and female samples separately
df_male   <- wvs7 |> filter(female == 0)
df_female <- wvs7 |> filter(female == 1)

# Layout: 1 row, 2 columns
par(mfrow = c(1, 2))

# Left panel (male)
plot(
  jitter(df_male$incomeLevel, amount = 0.3),
  jitter(df_male$equalIncentive, amount = 0.3),
  xlab = "Income level",
  ylab = "Egalitarian belief (outcome -> opportunity)",
  main = "Male"
)
abline(lm(equalIncentive ~ incomeLevel, data = df_male), col = "gold")

# Right panel (female)
plot(
  jitter(df_female$incomeLevel, amount = 0.3),
  jitter(df_female$equalIncentive, amount = 0.3),
  xlab = "Income level",
  ylab = "Egalitarian belief (outcome -> opportunity)",
  main = "Female"
)
abline(lm(equalIncentive ~ incomeLevel, data = df_female), col = "gold")

# Restore default layout: reset to 1 row, 1 column
par(mfrow = c(1, 1))
```

:::

---

- Women overall lean more toward equality of outcome (lower intercept), but the income–egalitarianism trend is consistent across genders
- Base graphics lacks a unified high-level grammar; fine-tuning is laborious → good for quick exploration, not final presentation

## Trellis Graphics

- Trellis Graph: split into small panels by a variable, with unified axes for comparison
- Proposed by statistician William Cleveland and colleagues in the 1980s–1990s
- The `lattice` package implements this in R: `xyplot(y ~ x | conditioning variable)`

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: trellisSetup

# Load the lattice package
library(lattice)

# Ensure the conditioning variable has clear labels
wvs7$female_label <- factor(
  wvs7$female,
  levels = c(FALSE, TRUE),
  labels = c("Male", "Female")
)
```

:::

---

- Draw with `xyplot(y ~ x | conditioning variable)`; a custom `panel` adds points plus a fitted line

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: trellisPlot

# Draw with xyplot (run the setup cell on the previous slide first)
xyplot(
  # Formula: y ~ x | conditioning variable
  jitter(equalIncentive, 0.5) ~ jitter(incomeLevel, 0.5) | female_label,
  data = wvs7,
  xlab = "Income level",
  ylab = "Egalitarian belief (outcome -> opportunity)",
  # Layout: 2 columns, 1 row
  layout = c(2, 1),
  # Custom panel function: define what each subpanel draws
  panel = function(x, y, ...) {
    panel.xyplot(x, y, ...)
    panel.lmline(x, y, col = "gold")
  }
)
```

:::

---

- Same effect as base graphics, but the code is cleaner and more uniform
- `lattice` can also draw 3D plots: `cloud(z ~ x * y)`

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: lattice3D

# Use cloud() to draw a 3D scatter cloud
cloud(jitter(equalIncentive, 0.5) ~ jitter(incomeLevel, 0.5) * jitter(education, 0.5), data = wvs7)
```

:::

- `cloud()` syntax `z ~ x * y`: z is height (dependent variable), x*y form the base plane
- Observe how "egalitarianism" undulates over the income × education plane

## Saving Output

- `lattice` returns a graphics object: store the object first, then `print()` to output
- Three steps: open device with `png()` → `print(p)` → close device with `dev.off()`

---

```r
# Save the scatter plot as an object
p <- xyplot(
  jitter(equalIncentive, 0.5) ~ jitter(incomeLevel, 0.5) | female_label,
  data = wvs7,
  xlab = "Income level",
  ylab = "Egalitarian belief (outcome -> opportunity)"
)

# Open the PNG graphics device
png("lattice_plot.png", width = 800, height = 600)

# Print the graphics object
print(p)

# Close the graphics device
dev.off()
```

## Exercise {.unnumbered background-color="#43464B"}

**Question 1**: Faceted scatter plot (lattice)

Use `xyplot()` to draw a scatter of `education` (x) against `equalIncentive` (y), faceted by `female_label`, with a **red** linear fit line; apply modest jitter to both axes.

::: { .hint exercise="latticeExercise" }

- Formula `y ~ x | conditioning variable`: y on the left of `~`, x on the right
- Use `jitter(variable, amount)` for jittering
- Inside `panel = function(x, y, ...)`, call `panel.xyplot()` + `panel.lmline(col = "red")`

:::

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: latticeExercise
#| exercise: latticeExercise

library(lattice)
data("wvs7", package = "drhur")

# Gender labels
wvs7$female_label <- factor(wvs7$female, levels = c(0, 1), labels = c("Male", "Female"))

# Complete the xyplot below

```

:::

::: { .solution exercise="latticeExercise" }

```r
library(lattice)

# Ensure gender labels are defined (if earlier code did not run)
wvs7$female_label <- factor(
  wvs7$female,
  levels = c(FALSE, TRUE),
  labels = c("Male", "Female")
)

# Draw the plot
xyplot(
  # Formula: y-variable ~ x-variable | conditioning variable
  # Use jitter() to spread overlapping points
  jitter(equalIncentive, 0.5) ~ jitter(education, 0.5) | female_label,
  data = wvs7,
  xlab = "Education level",
  ylab = "Egalitarian belief (outcome -> opportunity)",
  # Custom panel function
  panel = function(x, y, ...) {
    # Draw points
    panel.xyplot(x, y, ...)
    # Add a red linear fit line
    panel.lmline(x, y, col = "red")
  }
)

# Notes:
# - Left of ~ is y (equalIncentive), right is x (education); both jittered
# - The bar | female_label achieves "facet by gender"
# - Inside panel, first panel.xyplot() draws points, then panel.lmline(col="red") draws the red fit line
```

:::

## Visualization with `ggplot2` {.smaller}

- Trellis approach gradually showed limits in flexibility and extensibility
- Hadley Wickham introduced Wilkinson's Grammar of Graphics; `ggplot2` released in 2009
- Introduces layer system, facets, and unified themes
- Decomposes visualization into: data / mapping / geometric object / statistical transformation / scale / coordinate system

- Declarative paradigm: `ggplot()` binds data → `aes()` declares mappings → `geom_*()` specifies geometry → `+` stacks layers
- Focus on describing "how the data should appear"; let the program handle the drawing
- Three advantages:
    - Data sits at the core; all layers share the same data frame, making grouping/faceting natural
    - Statistical transformations are built in, no need to pre-compute, preserves the original data
    - Graphics objects can be saved and modified, preserving openness and flexibility

## Components and Grammar

- Mapping `mapping` (including `aes`, `alpha`, etc.): the "style" of a plot — color, shape, size
- Each aesthetic can be mapped to a variable or set as a constant (e.g., `color = "red"`)
- Geometric objects `geom_*`: the basic drawing units — points, lines, bars
- Not just static glyphs, but also statistical renderings (`geom_smooth` for smoothing, `geom_histogram` for histograms)
- Each `geom_*()` produces what we will call a "layer"

---

```r
# ggplot2 universal template
ggplot(data = <data>) +
  # Geometric layer 1
  geom_<shape1>(
    # Mapping: bind data variables to visual properties
    mapping = aes(x = <x-variable>, y = <y-variable>,
                  color = <border color>,
                  fill = <fill color>,
                  size  = <thickness/size>,
                  ......),
    # Setting: constant attributes that do not vary with data
    alpha  = <transparency, 0--1>
  ) +
  # Geometric layer 2 ...
  geom_<shape2>(...) +
  # Other components
  <scales, coordinates, themes>...
```

---

- Two notes on the template:
    - `data` can also be set inside `geom_*()` alone — useful when a layer uses a different subset/aggregation
    - `aes()` can sit inside `ggplot()` so layers inherit it; override inside a layer if needed

---

- Step 1: initialize the canvas, bind data and global mapping

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: ggplotInit

library(ggplot2)

# Initialize the canvas, bind data and global mapping
ggplot(wvs7, aes(x = incomeLevel, y = equalIncentive))
```

:::

---

- Step 2: stack jittered points with `geom_jitter()`
- `width`/`height` control jitter amount on x/y; `alpha` sets transparency to ease overlap

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: ggplotStep2

ggplot(wvs7, aes(x = incomeLevel, y = equalIncentive)) +
  # Add a jittered scatter layer
  geom_jitter(width = 0.3, height = 0.3, alpha = 0.7)
```

:::

---

- Step 3: add a linear regression fit line
- `method = "lm"` for linear model; `se = FALSE` turns off the confidence band

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: ggplotStep3

ggplot(wvs7, aes(x = incomeLevel, y = equalIncentive)) +
  geom_jitter(width = 0.3, height = 0.3, alpha = 0.7) +
  # Add a linear fit layer (method="lm")
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, color = "gold", linewidth = 1)
```

:::

---

- Step 4: fill in axis labels with `labs()`

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: ggplotStep4

ggplot(wvs7, aes(x = incomeLevel, y = equalIncentive)) +
  geom_jitter(width = 0.3, height = 0.3, alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, color = "gold", linewidth = 1) +
  # Add a labels layer; refine axis titles
  labs(x = "Income level", y = "Egalitarian belief (outcome -> opportunity)")
```

:::

---

- Facets need only `facet_wrap(~ female_label, ncol = 2)`: group by gender, two columns

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: ggplotFacet

# Ensure gender labels exist
wvs7$female_label <- factor(wvs7$female, levels = c(0, 1), labels = c("Male", "Female"))

ggplot(wvs7, aes(x = incomeLevel, y = equalIncentive)) +
  geom_jitter(width = 0.5, height = 0.5, alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE, color = "gold", linewidth = 1) +
  # Facet layer: facet by gender label, arranged in 2 columns
  facet_wrap(~ female_label, ncol = 2) +
  labs(x = "Income level", y = "Egalitarian belief (outcome -> opportunity)")
```

:::

---

- Trend consistent: income ↑ → slight lean toward equality of opportunity (true for both genders)
- Slight gender difference: women's fit line has a lower intercept → at the same income, women demand more equality of outcome

## Extension and Enrichment 

- Draw two more plots in different forms to feel the unified logic of the "universal template"
- First: a bar chart with confidence intervals, focused on **comparison**
- Comparing means is not enough; overlay error intervals to show "uncertainty"
- First aggregate: compute median and 95% confidence interval per income × gender group

---

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: dataBar

# Ensure gender labels exist
wvs7$female_label <- factor(wvs7$female, levels = c(0, 1), labels = c("Male", "Female"))

# Data prep: compute mean and confidence interval
wvs7_agg <- select(wvs7, equalIncentive, incomeLevel, female_label) |>
  # Drop missing values
  na.omit() |>
  # Group by income and gender
  group_by(incomeLevel, female_label) |>
  # Compute statistics
  summarise(median_ed = median(equalIncentive),
            sd_ed = sd(equalIncentive),
            se_ed = sd_ed / sqrt(n()),
            # Lower bound of 95% confidence interval
            lb_ed = median_ed - stats::qnorm(1 - (1 - 0.95) / 2) * se_ed,
            # Upper bound of 95% confidence interval
            ub_ed = median_ed + stats::qnorm(1 - (1 - 0.95) / 2) * se_ed)
```

:::

---

- `geom_bar(stat = "identity")` draws bars using the y values directly; `position_dodge()` places bars side by side
- `geom_errorbar()` draws error whiskers; position must match the bars

---

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: ggplotBar

# Initialize: x = income, y = median, fill mapped to gender
ggplot(wvs7_agg, aes(x = incomeLevel, y = median_ed, fill = female_label)) +
  # Bar chart: stat="identity" uses the y value directly; position_dodge places bars side by side
  geom_bar(stat = "identity",
          color = "black",
          position = position_dodge()) +
  # Error bars: map upper/lower bounds; position must match the bars (.9)
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = lb_ed, ymax = ub_ed),
                width = 0.2,
                position = position_dodge(.9),
                color = "darkred") +
  # Adjust labels and legend position
  labs(
    x = "Income level",
    y = "Egalitarian belief (outcome -> opportunity)"
  ) +
  theme(legend.position = "top") +
  # Customize fill colors and legend name
  scale_fill_gb(name = "Gender", reverse = TRUE)
```

:::

---

- Second: a heatmap — x/y represent two variables, color depth represents a third
- Especially fitting when the third variable is continuous (e.g., education `education`)
- First aggregate the median egalitarian belief for each education × income combination

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: dataHeat

# Data prep: compute median egalitarian belief for each education-income combination
wvs7 |>
  select(education, incomeLevel, equalIncentive) |>
  group_by(education, incomeLevel) |>
  summarise(equalIncentive = median(equalIncentive, na.rm=TRUE)) |>
  ungroup() -> wvs7_heat
```

:::

---

- `geom_tile()` draws tiles; `scale_fill_gb()` sets the continuous fill scale

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: ggplotHeat

# Initialize: x = education, y = income, fill mapped to egalitarian belief
ggplot(data = wvs7_heat, mapping = aes(
  y = incomeLevel, x = education, fill = equalIncentive)) +
  # Draw tiles
  geom_tile() +
  # Set labels
  labs(
    x = "Education level",
    y = "Income level"
  ) +
  # Set fill scale
  scale_fill_gb(name = "Egalitarian belief", discrete = FALSE)
```

:::

---

- Darker shades cluster top-right: highly educated + high-income individuals favor equality of opportunity most
- Consistent with the intuition that "the privileged prefer equality of opportunity"

## Exercise {.unnumbered background-color="#43464B"}

**Question 2**: Marginal-density scatter plot (ggside)

Use `ggside` to recreate a marginal-density scatter plot: income (x) × education (y) scatter (colored by `religious`) + a smoothing fit; overlay density plots above the x-axis and to the right of the y-axis. First drop rows with missing values in `incomeLevel`, `education`, and `religious`.

::: { .hint exercise="ggsideExercise" }

- Drop missing: `filter(if_all(c(...), ~ !is.na(.)))`
- Main plot: `geom_point(size = 2, alpha = 0.3)` + `geom_smooth(se = TRUE)`
- Marginals: `geom_xsidedensity()` / `geom_ysidedensity()`, `fill = religious`, `position = "stack"`

:::

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: ggsideExercise
#| exercise: ggsideExercise

library(ggside)
library(ggplot2)
data("wvs7", package = "drhur")

# Complete the ggside plot below

```

:::

::: { .solution exercise="ggsideExercise" }

```r
# Load the extension
library(ggside)

# Data preprocessing: drop rows with NA in any of the relevant variables
# if_all() checks that all specified columns are non-missing
wvs7_narm <- wvs7 |>
  filter(if_all(c(incomeLevel, education, religious), ~ !is.na(.)))

# Draw the plot
ggplot(data = wvs7_narm,
      aes(x = incomeLevel, y = education, color = religious)) +
    # Main scatter: tune size and alpha to handle overlap
    geom_point(size = 2, alpha = 0.3) +
    # Smoothing curve: aes(color = NULL) means do not group by religion, draw one overall trend line
    geom_smooth(aes(color = NULL), se=TRUE) +
    # X-axis marginal density (ggside feature)
    # after_stat(density) computes the density; position="stack" stacks the layers
    geom_xsidedensity(
        aes(y = after_stat(density), fill = religious),
        alpha = 0.5, size = 1, position = "stack"
    ) +
    # Y-axis marginal density
    geom_ysidedensity(
        aes(x = after_stat(density), fill = religious),
        alpha = 0.5, size = 1, position = "stack"
    ) +
    # Labels and theme
    labs(title = "Economy on Education" ,
         subtitle = "Scatter plot + density distribution",
         x = "Family Income", y = "Education") +
    # Adjust the proportion of the canvas occupied by the marginal plots
    theme(ggside.panel.scale = 0.4)

# Notes:
# - Inside geom_smooth, aes(color = NULL) overrides the global mapping to avoid drawing multiple colored fit lines
# - geom_x/ysidedensity are special ggside geometries that draw density plots along the margins
# - One plot shows correlation (scatter + line), distribution shape (marginal densities), and categorical heterogeneity (color) simultaneously
```

:::

## Exercise {.unnumbered background-color="#43464B"}

- The key to `ggplot2`: grasp the grammatical spine and the design philosophy
- Complex plots still follow the universal formula: declare data + mapping → stack `geom_*`, `labs()`, `scale_*`, `theme()`
- The fancier the look, the longer the code — but the underlying structure stays the same

::: {.callout-tip title="Extensions of `ggplot2`" .fragment}

- `ggplot2` has a large extension ecosystem:
    - `ggrepel`: avoids overlapping text labels in scatter plots
    - `ggsignif`: automatically adds significance-test markers
    - `gganimate`: makes plots "move" — produces animations
- Example: this book's `drhutools::cdplot()` draws conditional density plots

:::

---

::: {.fragment}

```{webr}
#| label: extCdplot

# Ensure gender labels exist
wvs7$female_label <- factor(wvs7$female, levels = c(0, 1), labels = c("Male", "Female"))

# Use cdplot() from the drhutools package
# Show how the male-to-female ratio varies across age groups
wvs7 |>
  select(variable = age, group = female_label) |>
  na.omit() |>
  drhutools::cdplot()
```

:::

- One line yields a professional conditional density plot — this is the charm of the `ggplot2` ecosystem

## Saving Output

- Saving from `ggplot2` is simplest: after drawing, run `ggsave("filename.png")`
- The suffix decides the format; use `width`, `height`, `path` to adjust size and location

```r
# Draw the plot
ggplot(data = wvs7_heat, mapping = aes(
  y = incomeLevel, x = education, fill = equalIncentive)) +
  geom_tile() +
  labs(
    x = "Education level",
    y = "Income level"
  ) +
  scale_fill_gb(name = "Egalitarian belief", discrete = FALSE)

# Save the most recently drawn plot as a PNG
ggsave("plot_heat.png")
```

## Final Reminders

- Online `ggplot2` code may run hundreds of lines — do not be intimidated
- The core is always the universal formula: declare data, declare mappings, add layers, then themes and scales
- Complex charts simply stack more layers on the same framework
- This chapter does not cover palettes, sizes, and other details — once the logic is mastered, self-learning is easy (you can even ask a large language model)
- The point of visualization is not to dazzle but to deliver the data's information to the reader accurately
- One principle: a good visualization delivers its key message to the reader's eyes at first glance

## Recap {.smaller}

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}

- Scatter plot: income level and egalitarian belief show a weak positive correlation
- Faceted plot shows: the trend is consistent across men and women, yet women favor equality of outcome more at the same income

- Base graphics
    - General plotting: `plot()`
    - Scatter jitter: `jitter()`
    - Linear fit line: `abline(lm(...))`
    - Panel layout: `par(mfrow = ...)`
- Trellis graphics
    - Conditional scatter plot: `xyplot()`
    - 3D scatter plot: `cloud()`

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

- ggplot2 visualization
    - Scatter layers: `geom_point()` / `geom_jitter()`
    - Fit line layer: `geom_smooth()`
    - Bar layer (with error bars): `geom_bar()` / `geom_errorbar()`
    - Heatmap layer: `geom_tile()`
    - Facets: `facet_wrap()`
    - Set theme: `theme_*`
    - Adjust scale: `scale_*`
    - Save plot: `ggsave()`

:::

::::
